2026-07-07 22:41:05  USER:             create a /docs directory in this project. We're going to build a
                                       project proposal and break it into tasks.

                                       The proposal:
                                       I believe there's a need for an application that will help me explore
                                       a bundle of information, with an LLM collaborating to explain it to
                                       me. People often do this, but the application and information are
                                       detached so there's either a lot of copying and pasting, or
                                       "bandwidth" of the communication is reduced because the LLM is
                                       working with the user's text, not the information directly.

                                       Examples:
                                       - Code Review of a PR
                                       - Joining a team & being given a lot of docs to read
                                       - Investigating a SEV, catching up on the in-progress data
                                       - Studying for an exam or interview

                                       My proposed solution:
                                       - Gather all the information in one place
                                       - Let an LLM explore it
                                       - LLM creates a custom application to explain it to you
                                       - The application is dynamic and interactive, not just a TLDR
                                       document

                                       LLMs like Claude do this today using Artifacts.
                                       This new application will be: Knowledge "Wiki", Artifact hosting,
                                       Intelligence, Realtime collaborative exploration, Multi-modal
                                       feedback, Progressive Refinement

                                       No need to invent the wheel:
                                       - a regular file directory for the Wiki
                                       - a web application for Artifact hosting
                                       - your already installed Claude Code for intelligence
                                       But the novel parts will be:
                                       - Better artifact generation through guidance
                                       - Better collaboration mechanisms to give feedback and get a better
                                       explanation app

                                       Phases:
                                       I'm going to start by building a simple application that lets an LLM
                                       create an artifact through tool use
                                       I'll add a simple chat to give feedback to the LLM and let it update
                                       the artifact
                                       I'll add multimodal operations to facilitate that conversation
                                       I'll introduce interactive data exploration elements to the artifacts
                                       I'll test and improve the "look and feel" of the application
                                       And if I have time, I'll improve its ability to ingest information
                                       into the "wiki"

                                       Based on the search results, here are the key findings about LLM
                                       agent file editing tool formats:
                                       ## Reliability Comparison
                                       Exact string matching is more reliable than line-number-based or
                                       unified-diff-based editing for LLM-generated patches. This finding
                                       has led to significant convergence in agent design: The
                                       str_replace_editor tool appears in 5 of 13 agents including
                                       OpenHands, SWE-agent, and others.
                                       last lines:
                                       **Unified Diff:** The standard unified format is unnatural for LLMs,
                                       and as patches grew larger and spread across hunks, the patcher
                                       struggled to keep track of line numbers.
                                       The research indicates that broader testing across diverse model
                                       families and agent systems is needed to better understand the impact
                                       of editing formats.

                                       GPT is terrible at working accurately with source code line numbers.

                                       editing formats, backed up by many quantitative benchmark
                                       experiments. Specifically regarding line numbers in unified diffs,
                                       GPT is frequently off-by-one, or labels a hunk as being line numbers

2026-07-07 22:50:15  ASSISTANT:        Done. The research gave a clear answer on the edit format, and I've
                                       updated the docs accordingly.

                                       **The edit-format research:** exact search/replace (`str_replace`
                                       style — send the exact `old_string` and its `new_string` replacement,
                                       require a unique match) is the clear winner for agent tool use:

                                       - A [benchmark of five file-editing strategies](https://dev.to/ceaksa
                                       n/i-benchmarked-5-file-editing-strategies-for-ai-coding-agents-heres-
                                       what-actually-works-1855) found search/replace had the highest solve
                                       rate — 23–27 percentage points above alternatives.
                                       - A [taxonomy of coding-agent
                                       architectures](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.03515) shows the ecosystem
                                       has converged on it: SWE-agent, OpenHands, Claude Code, and
                                       Anthropic's official text-editor tool all use `str_replace`, because
                                       exact string matching beats line numbers and unified diffs for LLM
                                       reliability.
                                       - [Aider's
                                       benchmarks](https://aider.chat/docs/more/edit-formats.html)
                                       established that models are "terrible at working accurately with line
                                       numbers"; even aider's [unified-diff
                                       experiment](https://aider.chat/docs/unified-diffs.html) had to strip
                                       line-number semantics to work.
                                       - Bonus for this project specifically: your intelligence layer is
                                       Claude Code, and Claude models are heavily post-trained on exactly
                                       this tool shape.

                                       **Doc updates:**

                                       - `ARCHITECTURE.md` — the wiki section now has a **Wiki API**
                                       subsection: list, chunked line reads (offset + limit, never
                                       whole-doc), create, rename, and `str_replace`-style edit, plus the
                                       Markdown-by-convention note. One nuance I encoded: chunked *reads*
                                       can include line numbers for orientation, but *edits* never reference
                                       them.
                                       - `docs/decisions.md` — created (as planned) with **D1**, recording
                                       the decision, the sources, the known failure mode to handle (a
                                       one-character mismatch must fail loudly, distinguishing "no match"
                                       from "multiple matches"), and when to revisit.
                                       - `tasks.md` — Phase 1 gained a wiki API task.
                                       - Worklog entry appended per AGENTS.md.

                                       Sources: [DEV editing-strategies benchmark](https://dev.to/ceaksan/i-
                                       benchmarked-5-file-editing-strategies-for-ai-coding-agents-heres-what
                                       -actually-works-1855), [Inside the Scaffold
                                       (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.03515), [aider edit
                                       formats](https://aider.chat/docs/more/edit-formats.html), [aider
                                       unified diffs](https://aider.chat/docs/unified-diffs.html)
2026-07-07 23:00:58  USER:             Back to the proposal:
                                       * I want to use the principle of "constrained generation" to make the
                                       LLM's artifact output more reliable
                                       * Rather than lettign it invent anything out of any HTML, with
                                       varying results, I want to give it a vocabulary for building an
                                       exploration app
                                       * The application will be part authoring, part exploration/learning.
                                       IOW, the author is also the intended learner. Where the application
                                       doesn't help the author learn, they can ask it to refine or re-do
                                       * We'll balance the power of raw HTML generation with the discipline
                                       of pre-created components
                                       * I believe OpenUI is a good way to serve HTML and more importantly,
                                       make it quickly & easily editable vs. raw edits to HTML - this is an
                                       experiment in defining an HTML-editing protocol.

                                       what OpenUI refers to so the docs reference it accurately, then fold
                                       everything in.

                                       constrained generation and OpenUI concretely: the component library
                                       is the contract for what the LLM may emit, and revisions become small
                                       edits to a structured line-oriented representation instead of surgery
                                       on an HTML blob. The "artifact format" open decision is narrowed to
                                       the details (initial vocabulary, on-disk representation, escape-hatch
                                       sandboxing).

                                       (constrained vocabulary + structured edits) on a different
                                       representation if OpenUI fights you, and revisit triggers (constant
                                       escape-hatch use; conflicts with the Phase 4 runtime API).
                                       - **`tasks.md`** — Phase 1 now starts with evaluating/integrating
                                       OpenUI and defining the initial vocabulary; Phase 4's guidance task
                                       became "grow the vocabulary," fed by escape-hatch usage.

                                       I verified the OpenUI reference:
                                       [openui.com](https://www.openui.com/) is the generative-UI standard
                                       whose component libraries (schemas + React renderers) generate the
                                       system prompt constraining what the LLM emits — which is precisely
                                       your "vocabulary" idea, so the fit is real, not just branding.

                                       One connection worth noticing: the project now has *two* editing
                                       protocols by design — `str_replace` for wiki files (D1) and OpenUI's
                                       structured edits for artifacts (D2). Same underlying question (how
                                       should an LLM revise text reliably?), answered differently for prose
                                       vs. UI. That contrast might itself be one of the more interesting
                                       findings of the experiment.

                                       Sources: [OpenUI](https://www.openui.com/), [OpenUI
                                       docs](https://www.openui.com/docs)

2026-07-08 00:12:03  ASSISTANT:        Welcome back — logged the resume at 17:11, so your two-hour window
                                       runs to about 19:11.

                                       My recommendation: **a thin vertical slice that goes through OpenUI**
                                       — one path from wiki to rendered artifact, built directly on the
                                       experimental medium. Concretely:

                                       1. Scaffold a minimal web app with the OpenUI runtime in it (OpenUI's
                                       React runtime mostly makes the stack decision for you — Next.js or
                                       Vite + React).
                                       2. Define a tiny vocabulary — three or four components, just enough
                                       to express a simple explanation (say, a card, a section, a callout, a
                                       code block).
                                       3. Have Claude Code read a small sample wiki and write an artifact in
                                       OpenUI's format to a file.
                                       4. Render it in the app.

                                       The reasoning: of everything in TASKS.md, **D2 is the load-bearing
                                       unknown**. OpenUI is explicitly an experiment, and its outcome shapes
                                       almost everything downstream — the web stack, the artifact tool's
                                       shape, the on-disk representation, and eventually the editing
                                       protocol that's the heart of the project. If OpenUI turns out
                                       awkward, you want to know that in the first session, not after you've
                                       built a chat pipeline and wiki API around it. The slice above is
                                       simultaneously the Phase 1 walking skeleton *and* the D2 spike — no
                                       throwaway work either way.

                                       What I'd deliberately **not** start with:

                                       - **The wiki API** — it's the best-understood, lowest-risk work on
                                       the board (five CRUD-ish endpoints with a decided edit format). It'll
                                       be just as easy in week two, and nothing in the vertical slice needs
                                       it yet — Claude Code can read the wiki with its own file tools for
                                       now.
                                       - **Chat/feedback** — Phase 2 by design, and its shape depends on how
                                       artifact editing works, which is exactly what the spike answers.
                                       - **Generation guidance** — meaningful only once there's a vocabulary
                                       to guide toward.

                                       The one question worth settling before the slice (it's a fork in the
                                       road, not a detail): **how you drive Claude Code** — Agent SDK versus
                                       headless CLI. For the spike itself you can dodge it — run Claude Code
                                       interactively in a terminal and let the web app just watch the
                                       artifact file — and honestly that dodge might be the fastest way to
                                       get to "I can see a generated artifact render" today.

                                       What were your ideas? If they differ, I'm curious where — you may be
                                       weighting a risk I'm not seeing.

2026-07-08 00:17:52  ASSISTANT:        This is a great specimen — it's essentially a hand-built version of
                                       exactly what the product should generate. Here's the decomposition.

                                       ## What the artifact is

                                       "PR 502764 Explainer" — a six-chapter interactive walkthrough of a
                                       billing refactor. Structurally it's ~930 lines of HTML, then ~550
                                       lines of JS that's mostly *data* (per-section content objects) plus
                                       small render functions. Notably, it already assumes a shared runtime
                                       (`framework.css`/`framework.js`, `window.Explainer.store`) and
                                       declares its interactive state in a JSON manifest
                                       (`#explainer-state`) so a *host* can drive state changes the same way
                                       clicks do.

                                       ## Section decomposition vs. the current vocabulary (`Stack`, `Tabs`,
                                       `Content`)

                                       | Artifact section | Structure | Current-vocabulary fit |
                                       |---|---|---|
                                       | Header | Title/subtitle + **context picker** (4 reader levels: new
                                       / billing / flow / audit) that swaps prose page-wide | `Content` +
                                       hand-rolled JS — no component for it |
                                       | Chapter nav (6 chapters) | Tab bar → one visible chapter |
                                       **`Tabs`** ✓ — the one real match |
                                       | 1. Orientation | Labeled rows (Summary / Components / Code Shape /
                                       Local Code), a nested **side-nav gallery** with an SVG code-shape
                                       diagram, plus an **aside** (Distinct Changes / Not Changed / file
                                       changelist) | All `Content` escape hatch |
                                       | 2. Concepts | Grid of definition boxes + a **gallery**: glossary
                                       term list → detail pane (title, context-sensitive body,
                                       mini-artifact) | `Content` |
                                       | 3. Flow | Context-variant intro paragraphs + **gallery** of 5 flow
                                       steps | `Content` |
                                       | 4. Outcomes | A shared **timeline diagram** (rows of spans/markers)
                                       + legend + **gallery** of 7 outcome cases, each with its own
                                       mini-timeline | `Content` |
                                       | 5. Callouts | Repeated **callout** card: heading + severity
                                       **badge**, prose, then an evidence block — before/after pseudocode
                                       pair, a 4-step rail of cards, or a two-column split | `Content` |
                                       | 6. Evidence | **Gallery** of 6 test-proofs rendered as
                                       given/when/then mini-timelines + a closing "compact review question"
                                       | `Content` |

                                       So today: `Stack` holds the page, `Tabs` gets the chapters, and
                                       **~95% of the artifact falls through to the raw-HTML escape hatch** —
                                       which, per D2, is exactly the signal for what the vocabulary needs
                                       next.

                                       ## What the artifact says the vocabulary is missing

                                       In priority order by how hard the page leans on each:

                                       1. **Gallery / master-detail** — side-nav item list driving a detail
                                       pane (title + body + embedded artifact). Used *five times*; it's the
                                       workhorse of the whole explainer.
                                       2. **Context/audience switcher + context-variant text** — one global
                                       state ("how much does the reader already know?") with every prose
                                       block able to carry per-context variants. This is your
                                       author-is-the-learner principle rendered as a component.
                                       3. **Callout card** — title, severity badge (`risk`/`watch`/`safe`),
                                       body, evidence slot.
                                       4. **Labeled stack rows** (label/body pairs) and the **aside/context
                                       panel** (changed vs. not-changed, file changelist).
                                       5. **Comparison blocks** — before/after pseudocode pair, two-column
                                       split, step rail.
                                       6. **Timeline diagram** — rows of spans and markers with notes. More
                                       domain-specific; plausibly stays escape-hatch (like the inline SVG
                                       diagram) until it recurs across artifacts.

                                       ## The most interesting finding

                                       The `#explainer-state` manifest + central store is a protocol
                                       feature, not a component: the document declares its state keys (with
                                       initial values and human-readable comments), all interactivity flows
                                       through the store, and "a host-driven set-state message takes exactly
                                       the same path as a local click." That's precisely the mechanism Phase
                                       4 needs — interaction signals flowing out to the LLM, and the LLM (or
                                       chat) steering the artifact by writing state. Worth capturing in the
                                       docs as a design input for the OpenUI integration: components should
                                       declare named state, and the host should be able to
                                       read/subscribe/write it.

                                       Want me to record this analysis in `docs/` (e.g., as a design-input
                                       note feeding the vocabulary task in TASKS.md)?

                                       needed.

                                       Finally, create in docs a .oui file (openui) with this explainer in
                                       your best attempt at OpenUI format.

2026-07-08 00:25:53  ASSISTANT:        Now the server-side schema mirror in `server/src/ui-library.ts` (same

2026-07-08 00:25:59  USER:             [Request interrupted by user]
2026-07-08 00:26:06  USER:             wait, it looks like you're building a TSX - why?
2026-07-08 00:26:22  ASSISTANT:        Fair question — here's the reasoning: in the scaffold your other
                                       agents built, an OpenUI component is two synced halves. The contract
                                       in `client/src/lib/openui.tsx` is a Zod schema *plus a React
                                       renderer* — OpenUI's runtime is React, so a component only exists on
                                       screen if something maps its props to DOM. The server side
                                       (`server/src/ui-library.ts`) is deliberately schema-only (`component:
                                       null`) because it just generates the system prompt; the file headers
                                       in both say they must stay in sync.

                                       So "create the gallery, aside, and comparison as components" meant,
                                       in this codebase: add schema + renderer to the client library (the
                                       TSX I was editing), then mirror the schemas in the server library so
                                       the LLM's prompt knows about them. The TSX isn't a detour — it's
                                       where the vocabulary's rendering half lives.

                                       That said, if your intent was different — e.g., you only wanted the
                                       component *schemas/contracts* designed for now (leaving rendering to
                                       your other agents), or you'd rather renderers live somewhere else —
                                       say the word and I'll back the TSX changes out and just do the
                                       schema/mirror + `.oui` file. Otherwise I'll continue: server mirror
                                       next, then a typecheck, then the `.oui` translation of the explainer.

2026-07-08 00:31:39  ASSISTANT:        All three deliverables are done, and both library halves typecheck
                                       clean.

                                       **D3 recorded** — `docs/decisions.md` now has "Component state lives
                                       in a hierarchical key-value store": artifacts declare state keys in a
                                       manifest (initial value + description), components subscribe and
                                       render from the store, and host-driven writes take the same path as
                                       clicks. The why-list captures what you'll use it for later:
                                       interaction signals to the LLM, LLM steering of the UI, state-tagged
                                       screenshots, and free session persistence. ARCHITECTURE.md links to
                                       it.

                                       **Three components added** to both `client/src/lib/openui.tsx`
                                       (schema + React renderer) and `server/src/ui-library.ts` (schema
                                       mirror), registered in both libraries, with `Tabs`/`Stack` child
                                       unions widened to accept them:

                                       - **`Gallery`** — master-detail: left nav (label + small
                                       description), right detail pane (title + content). It takes an
                                       optional `stateKey` prop (e.g. `"flow/selected-step"`) so the LLM
                                       names the selection state now, even though selection is local
                                       `useState` until the D3 store lands.
                                       - **`Aside`** — main column plus a narrow panel of titled context
                                       blocks.
                                       - **`Comparison`** — 2+ equal-width labeled panels.

                                       **`docs/pr-review.oui`** — the full explainer in OpenUI Lang, ~90
                                       statements. The six chapters are a `Tabs`; all five side-nav boards
                                       became `Gallery`s keeping their original store keys; the before/after
                                       code pairs, split panels, given/when/then proof grids, and the
                                       four-step ordering rail all became `Comparison`s; orientation's frame
                                       became `Aside`. Content prose carried over verbatim (the "new to this
                                       code" variants).

                                       Two honest findings from the translation, both now tracked in
                                       TASKS.md:

                                       1. **What the vocabulary lost:** the context/audience switcher — the
                                       original's four prose variants per block have no component, so the
                                       `.oui` flattens to one audience. That plus wiring `stateKey` to a
                                       real store are the two highest-value next vocabulary items.
                                       2. **What got simplified rather than lost:** the styled timeline
                                       diagrams depended on the original's framework CSS, so I rendered them
                                       as self-contained `<pre>` ASCII timelines and one inline SVG inside
                                       `Content`. Readable, but a native `Timeline` component is the
                                       eventual answer if the pattern recurs.

                                       Time check: you're at ~20 minutes into this session's two-hour
                                       window.

2026-07-08 00:46:20  ASSISTANT:        Housekeeping done — old `.oui` removed, D3's provenance note updated
                                       (interestingly, the state-store manifest turns out to be what your
                                       company's hosting conversion *added*; the original uses plain DOM
                                       state — so D3 is us keeping the good part of that conversion),
                                       tracker and worklog updated.

                                       Now your question: what's still missing for a faithful recreation of
                                       `pr-502764-review.html`. I compared the rendered result we'd get
                                       today against the original, in rough priority order:

                                       **1. A context switcher — the one real functional gap.** The gating
                                       mechanism now exists (any component can say `context: ["audit"]`),
                                       but nothing lets the *reader* change level: the original has that
                                       header dropdown ("New to this code" / "Know billing basics" / …)
                                       driving everything. We need a `ContextPicker` component that sets the
                                       app's context level (eventually just a store write to the `context`
                                       key per D3; until then, a callback the app shell provides). Without
                                       it the context variants are dead weight.

                                       **2. Style options on `Comparison` — the biggest visual-fidelity
                                       lever.** You guessed right. The original has four distinct
                                       side-by-side looks that our one uniform bordered-card rendering
                                       flattens:
                                       - `snippet-pair`: one outer border, internal dividers, header strips
                                       - `split` / `before-after`: no boxes at all — just a top border per
                                       column
                                       - the ordering `rail`: cards with `>` arrows between steps

                                       A single `variant` prop (`"cards" | "divided" | "bare" | "rail"`)
                                       covers all four. `Gallery` needs less: a `navWidth` option (the
                                       original varies 240–300px per board) and accent styling on the active
                                       nav item. `Aside` is close enough already.

                                       **3. Page-level look: this is a scoping decision, not a component.**
                                       The original is a designed *document* — dark background, its own type
                                       scale, 1500px max-width page padding. Our artifact panel imposes the
                                       app-shell theme. A `<style>` in a `Content` block can target artifact
                                       classes (that's what the `.oui`'s `styles` statement does) but
                                       letting it style `body` would leak into the app shell. The clean
                                       answer is style isolation for the artifact panel (shadow DOM or
                                       iframe) so an artifact can bring a whole theme — and that's the same
                                       decision as the "Content sandboxing" task already on the board. Until
                                       then, artifacts inherit the app theme and the raw-HTML fallback
                                       covers interiors, which is a legible compromise.

                                       **4. Translation completeness, no new components needed.** The
                                       original swaps *every* board's detail text per context level
                                       (concepts, flow, orientation-code bodies each have four variants).
                                       Our Gallery item content already accepts gated `Content`s, so full
                                       fidelity is just mechanical expansion of the `.oui` — roughly 3–4×
                                       more body statements. I demonstrated the pattern on the orientation
                                       summary and flow intro; the rest is grind, not design.

                                       **5. Already covered, for the record:** all interactive behavior in
                                       the original (chapter tabs, board selection, diagram show/hide) maps
                                       onto existing components; the timeline/snippet/pseudo artifacts are
                                       now carried verbatim with their CSS.

                                       My suggestion for order: ContextPicker first (it unblocks the feature
                                       you built the document around), Comparison variants second, and fold
                                       page-theming into the sandboxing decision rather than solving it ad
                                       hoc. Want me to build the first two?

2026-07-08 00:54:39  ASSISTANT:        Done — `docs/pr-502764-review.oui` is now everything the current
                                       vocabulary can express, about 200 statements. What changed in this
                                       pass:

                                       **Every context variant from the archetype is now in the file.** The
                                       original swaps body text per reader level in far more places than the
                                       few I'd demonstrated; all of them are now gated `Content` statements:

                                       - Orientation: summary/components (4 variants), each of the 5
                                       code-shape gallery items, and the "Local Code" section including its
                                       four different `local-points` grids per level
                                       - Concepts: all 5 glossary terms' bodies
                                       - Flow: the intro plus all 5 steps' bodies
                                       - The audit-only paragraphs in Callouts and Evidence

                                       Where the original text is identical across levels I merged rather
                                       than duplicated — e.g. `context: ["flow", "audit"]` — which the
                                       multi-level prop was designed for. The "Overview" code-shape body is
                                       identical across all four levels in the original, so it stays
                                       ungated.

                                       **What a viewer will see:** with a context level set, this should now
                                       track the original chapter-for-chapter — same prose, same verbatim
                                       timeline/snippet/pseudo markup with its CSS. With *no* level set, all
                                       variants render stacked (the documented default). That makes the
                                       missing `ContextPicker` the thing standing between "content-complete"
                                       and "actually usable as intended" — until it exists, the app shell
                                       would need to pass `contextLevel` into `GenerativeView` some other
                                       way.

                                       **Still out of reach without new components** (unchanged from the gap
                                       list): the header's context dropdown, the four distinct comparison
                                       stylings (`snippet-pair` borders vs. bare `split` columns vs. the
                                       rail arrows — our one Comparison look approximates them), and the
                                       page-level dark theme/type scale, which is the artifact
                                       style-isolation question tied to the sandboxing task.

                                       One honest caveat: I've matched the `.oui` against the archetype by
                                       construction, but nobody has rendered it yet — the first real render
                                       will likely surface OpenUI Lang parsing details (multi-prop object
                                       syntax, `\n` handling in strings) worth checking before building more
                                       on top.
2026-07-08 00:55:17  USER:             why are the comparison stylings out of reach without new components?
2026-07-08 00:55:53  ASSISTANT:        Fair catch — they're not, and my claim was imprecise. Let me restate
                                       it correctly.

                                       What's actually true: they're out of reach *while using the
                                       `Comparison` component*, because its chrome is hardcoded in the
                                       renderer — every panel gets the same rounded border, padding, and
                                       gap. The four original looks differ exactly in that chrome
                                       (snippet-pair: one outer border with internal dividers and header
                                       strips; split: no boxes, just a top rule per column; before-after:
                                       outer border with a center divider; rail: cards joined by `>`
                                       arrows). The component has no prop to vary any of that, so anything
                                       rendered through `Comparison` comes out in the one house style.
                                       That's the sense in which a new *component feature* (a `variant`
                                       prop) is needed.

                                       But you already gave me the out, and I under-used it: **raw HTML
                                       fallback**. The original comparisons are just markup, and the
                                       `.oui`'s `styles` block already carries the CSS for all four patterns
                                       (`.snippet-pair`, `.split`, `.before-after`, `.pseudo`, even
                                       `.mark`). So a single `Content` statement with the verbatim `<div
                                       class='snippet-pair'>…</div>` reproduces the original exactly, today,
                                       no new components.

                                       The reason I reached for `Comparison` anyway was a deliberate trade,
                                       and it's worth making explicit because it's the core D2 tension:

                                       - **`Comparison`** gives *semantic structure* — the LLM and host know
                                       "these are two labeled alternatives," which is what makes future
                                       editing, state, and guidance work. Costs pixel fidelity.
                                       - **`Content` fallback** gives *pixel fidelity* — and costs
                                       structure: to the system it's an opaque HTML blob again.

                                       For a vocabulary benchmark I biased toward structure, then
                                       over-claimed by calling the styling "out of reach" instead of "not
                                       expressible through the component yet."

                                       So you have three real options for the `.oui` right now:

                                       1. **Flip the comparison sites to raw `Content`** with original
                                       markup — full visual fidelity today (the escape hatch doing its
                                       documented job: heavy use of it marks exactly where the vocabulary
                                       needs to grow).
                                       2. **Leave them as `Comparison`** — structurally honest, visually
                                       approximate, and the gap becomes the motivation for the `variant`
                                       prop.
                                       3. **Hybrid** — fallback for the distinctive ones (snippet-pair,
                                       rail), keep `Comparison` where the house style is close enough
                                       (given/when/then, the two-column splits).

                                       Say which and I'll make the edit — or if you'd rather just add the
                                       `variant` prop to `Comparison` now and keep the structure, that's a
                                       small component change, not a new component.

                                       other purposes - no padding, etc. that might break other comparison
                                       renderings

2026-07-08 01:03:15  ASSISTANT:        Both done.

                                       **Files moved** — `pr-502764-review.html` (the archetype) and
                                       `pr-review.html` (the hosted conversion) are now in `docs/` alongside
                                       the `.oui`.

                                       **Comparison generalized** — the design now follows "layout from
                                       props, look from content":

                                       - **Unstyled by default**: no padding, no borders, no gap, no rounded
                                       cards — just equal-width grid columns. Panel content fully controls
                                       its own appearance, so nothing the component adds can fight a
                                       rendering it wasn't designed for.
                                       - **Opt-in layout props**: `gap` (any CSS value), `border` (outer),
                                       `dividers` (vertical rules between panels).
                                       - **Labels optional** — a panel can omit its heading entirely and
                                       bring its own inside the content.
                                       - **CSS hooks instead of variants**: the wrapper always carries class
                                       `comparison` plus an optional `className` prop; panels carry
                                       `comparison-panel`; labels render as `h3.comparison-label`. So an
                                       artifact stylesheet can restyle any instance — which is more general
                                       than a fixed `variant` enum would have been.

                                       The `.oui` now exercises this for all four archetype looks: the four
                                       flow snippet-pairs use `{className: "cmp-snippets", border: true,
                                       dividers: true}` with the label restyled into the original header
                                       strip; the before/after tables use `cmp-before-after` with dividers;
                                       the splits and proof grid use gap-based classes; and the callout rail
                                       uses `cmp-rail` with a 22px gap and the original `>` arrows restored
                                       via `::after`. All eleven call sites converted, both library halves
                                       typecheck.

                                       One consequence worth knowing: any *other* artifact that uses
                                       `Comparison` bare will now get plain unpadded columns instead of the
                                       old bordered cards — that's the intended contract (bring your own
                                       chrome), and the component description tells the LLM exactly which
                                       hooks exist for styling.
2026-07-08 01:09:30  USER:             create a design principle: the only reason to use specialized
                                       components is if they offer an advantage over raw HTML being
                                       rendered. The structural components offer a boundary to contain
                                       content and easily edit just a part of the page. There's nothing
                                       special about the Comparison component - it's just a side-by-side
                                       display that hosts two children we could independently edit.  So the
                                       principle is that OpenUI structural components are just a named
                                       editing point and don't need any special behaviors or styling.

2026-07-08 01:10:43  ASSISTANT:        Recorded in three places:

                                       - **`proposal.md`** — a new design principle, "Structural components
                                       are named editing points," alongside the constrained-generation
                                       principle it sharpens: the vocabulary's value is reliability *of
                                       editing*, and the only justification for a component over raw HTML is
                                       an advantage raw HTML can't offer — for structural ones, that's
                                       solely the independently-editable boundary.
                                       - **`decisions.md` D4** — the fuller version with rationale
                                       (edit-mode revisions want small named statements; component chrome
                                       fights content — the Comparison rework is cited as the proof) and
                                       consequences: neutral layout only, stable CSS hooks + `className`,
                                       and behavior must be *earned* (Gallery selection qualifies because
                                       navigation state is structural and becomes a D3 store key; the
                                       `context` gate qualifies; nothing else does).
                                       - **`ARCHITECTURE.md`** — a cross-reference in the artifacts section.

                                       Two follow-ups this principle implies, which I've noted but not done:

                                       1. **Gallery, Aside, and Tabs now violate D4's letter** — Gallery has
                                       opinionated nav-button styling (accent borders, hover states), Aside
                                       draws muted boxes around its blocks, Tabs inherits the shadcn look.
                                       By the same treatment Comparison got, they should shed default chrome
                                       and expose hook classes (`gallery-nav`, `aside-block`, etc.). That's
                                       a real code change worth its own pass.
                                       2. **The generation guidance should teach the principle** — a rule in
                                       the server's system prompt telling the LLM "structural components
                                       create edit boundaries; raw HTML creates appearance," so generated
                                       artifacts follow D4 rather than expecting components to look like
                                       anything.

                                       Say the word on either and I'll take them; otherwise the principle is
                                       on the books.

2026-07-08 01:19:12  ASSISTANT:        Both fixes are in, with one coordination note worth knowing about.

                                       **1. The vocabulary now fully follows D4.** Gallery, Aside, and Tabs
                                       got the same treatment as Comparison:

                                       - **Gallery** — plain buttons instead of shadcn Buttons, no
                                       colors/borders/hover; hook classes `gallery`, `gallery-nav`,
                                       `gallery-nav-item` (+ `active` and `data-active`), `gallery-detail`,
                                       `gallery-title`; new `navWidth`, `gap`, `className` props. The only
                                       remaining default affordances are cursor, text alignment, and a bold
                                       selected label.
                                       - **Aside** — the muted boxes are gone; blocks are bare `aside-block`
                                       divs with `h3.aside-block-title` headings; new `asideWidth`, `gap`,
                                       `className` props.
                                       - **Tabs** — rebuilt without shadcn entirely: own selection state,
                                       `tabs-nav` / `tabs-trigger` (+ `active`) / `tabs-panel` hooks, proper
                                       tablist ARIA, `className` prop. Only the active panel renders.

                                       **2. Generation guidance teaches the principle.** The system prompt
                                       now has a D4 rule: structural components are named editing points
                                       rendering neutral layout only — decompose into many small named
                                       statements, and bring the look via a `<style>` Content block
                                       targeting the hook classes.

                                       Both library halves (client renderers + server schema mirror) are in
                                       sync and typecheck.

                                       **The coordination note:** while I worked, another of your agents
                                       normalized `pr-502764-review.oui` to **positional-argument syntax** —
                                       which reveals the real OpenUI Lang convention: args in schema order
                                       with `null` for skipped optionals, e.g. `Comparison([...], null,
                                       true, true, "cmp-snippets")`. They preserved my Comparison options in
                                       that form, and I followed the same convention for the rest: all five
                                       galleries now pass `"300px"` nav width, the orientation Aside passes
                                       `"300px", "22px"`, and the `.oui` stylesheet gained
                                       archetype-faithful chrome for the newly bare components — the boxed
                                       chapter tab bar, the bordered nav items with the `>` affordance and
                                       accent active state, and the aside's ruled blocks. One consequence of
                                       positional syntax to keep in mind: adding new optional props to a
                                       schema is only safe *at the end* of the prop order, or every existing
                                       artifact's positional args shift — a real versioning constraint on
                                       the vocabulary worth remembering when it grows.
2026-07-08 01:22:07  USER:             pretty good. but it needs a <styles> content block at the top so it

2026-07-08 01:22:58  ASSISTANT:        Right — and it's even a bit deeper than that: the `.oui` *has* a
                                       `styles` Content block, but it only styles the artifact's own classes
                                       (timelines, snippets, nav hooks). The raw HTML *elements* — `h2`,

                                       app's Tailwind preflight resets all browser defaults (headings

                                       archetype got those from its `body`-level rules, which I never
                                       carried over since a bare `h2 { … }` rule in the styles block would
                                       leak into the app shell (chat pane included).

                                       The clean fix follows D4: give **Stack** its hook class so the
                                       artifact root is targetable, then scope base typography under it.

2026-07-08 01:24:07  RESULT:           The file /Users/steve/projects/explore/docs/worklog-2026-07-07.md has

2026-07-08 01:24:20  ASSISTANT:        Exactly right — and the reason it was broken is worth spelling out:
                                       the app's Tailwind preflight resets every element default, so without
                                       base rules an `<h2>` renders as plain inline-weight text and `<p>`
                                       loses its margins. The archetype solved this with `body`-level
                                       typography rules, but a bare `h2 { … }` in a Content `<style>` block
                                       would leak into the app shell and restyle the chat pane too.

                                       So the fix has two parts:

                                       1. **`Stack` now carries the hook class `stack`** (plus a `className`
                                       prop, matching the other components) — since Stack is the artifact
                                       root, `.stack` is a leak-free scope for artifact-wide CSS. Its
                                       component description tells the LLM the pattern explicitly: the host
                                       resets element defaults, so include a `<style>` Content block with
                                       base typography scoped under `.stack`. Both library halves updated
                                       and typechecking.

                                       2. **The `.oui` stylesheet now opens with the archetype's base
                                       typography**, scoped: `.stack h1/h2/h3` sizes and weights, `.stack p`
                                       margins and muted color, `code` font stack, accent links, and
                                       `dl`/`dt`/`dd` rules for the summary lists. The CSS variables also
                                       moved up to `.stack`, so every descendant inherits them.

                                       One nuance I kept out deliberately: the archetype's `body {
                                       background: #101214 }`. Scoping that to `.stack` would make the
                                       artifact panel force-dark regardless of app theme — that's the
                                       page-theming/style-isolation question we already parked with the
                                       sandboxing task, and the light-scheme variable overrides handle
                                       readability meanwhile.

                                       With this, the render chain is complete: components provide structure
                                       and hooks, the styles block provides both the artifact-class CSS
                                       *and* the element baseline, all contained under `.stack`.

2026-07-08 01:41:18  ASSISTANT:        Both changes are in, and the model is now:

                                       - **Levels are integers.** Level **0 always exists** and is what the
                                       app renders at (`DEFAULT_CONTEXT_LEVEL = 0`, `GenerativeView` takes
                                       `contextLevel?: number`). An exploration that wants more depths
                                       introduces 1, 2, 3… and gates its components to them knowingly.
                                       - **No `context` prop → always visible.** Gated components render
                                       only when the active level is in their list. Schemas on both halves
                                       and the generation prompt rule teach exactly this, including "put 0
                                       on the variant that should show by default."

                                       The `.oui` converted cleanly — 63 gated statements mapped `new→[0]`,
                                       `billing→[1]`, `flow→[2]`, `audit→[3]` (with the shared-text variants
                                       becoming `[2, 3]`). Everything typechecks, and the rendering behavior
                                       you saw after the last fix is preserved: level 0 shows the beginner
                                       prose, everything else waits for a switcher.

                                       One thing this simplification deliberately drops, worth remembering
                                       when the context-switcher component gets built: the archetype's
                                       levels were *named* ("Know billing basics", "Checking invariants"),
                                       and numbers alone can't label a picker. The natural home for that
                                       mapping is the artifact's state manifest when D3 lands — the
                                       exploration declares `context: {initial: 0, levels: ["New to this
                                       code", "Know billing basics", …]}` and the switcher renders labels
                                       while components gate on integers. I left that as part of the
                                       existing switcher task rather than inventing it now.

                                       levels.

                                       **What exceeded the proposal:** a design-principles layer it never
                                       anticipated. Four recorded decisions (D1 str_replace, D2 OpenUI, D3
                                       state store, D4 editing points), each with real evidence behind it
                                       rather than taste — D4 in particular emerged from watching
                                       Comparison's hardcoded chrome fight content it should have hosted.
                                       The benchmark methodology (translate a real work artifact into the
                                       vocabulary) turned out to be the engine of the day: it forced every
                                       component decision and validated rendering fidelity end-to-end. Two
                                       practical findings you didn't have this morning: the positional-args
                                       versioning constraint on the vocabulary, and the
                                       host-CSS-reset/`.stack`-scoping pattern.

                                       **The honest gap:** the core hypothesis is still untested. Everything
                                       proven so far shows the pipeline can *render and revise* an
                                       explanation app. Nothing yet shows the LLM can *author a good one
                                       from a wiki of raw material* — the benchmark was hand-translated from
                                       a finished explainer, not generated from source. That test is cheap
                                       and is my top recommendation for next session: drop the PR diff
                                       behind the archetype into a sample wiki, ask the in-app Claude for an
                                       exploration, and grade it against the hand-built version. The wiki
                                       pillar (API endpoints, sample wikis) is the other untouched corner.

                                       **Process note worth keeping:** the docs discipline paid for itself —
                                       every design change today traces to a recorded decision — and the
                                       multi-agent setup genuinely parallelized (workers landed phases 2–3
                                       plumbing while we did vocabulary), with the one collision resolving
                                       in a way that taught us about the parser.

                                       Good two hours. Enjoy the break.

                                       sidebar.

                                       components (Stack, Content, Tabs, Gallery, Aside, Comparison) with
                                       hook classes and layout props, mirrored across client renderers and
                                       the server's prompt-generating schemas.
                                       - **The context-level system**: integer levels, 0 always present,
                                       per-component gating, prompt rules teaching the convention.
                                       - **The benchmark artifact**: `pr-502764-review.oui` (~200
                                       statements) — the work explainer expressed in the vocabulary,

                                       overlay; screenshot round-trip into chat as image content blocks
                                       (verified: the LLM described the annotated view back).
                                       - **Safety and observability**: sandboxed server LLM (MCP-only file
                                       access), JSONL event log covering back-end and front-end events,
                                       front-end-state tool for the LLM (open file, cursor/selection).
                                       - **The docs corpus**: proposal, architecture, decision log D1–D4,
                                       phase roadmap, live task tracker, timestamped worklog, this review.

                                       ### Techniques

                                       - **Benchmark-driven design** — translate a real artifact into the
                                       medium; every friction point becomes a component decision;
                                       escape-hatch usage marks vocabulary gaps.
                                       - **Docs-first, decisions-as-records** — each design choice logged
                                       with rationale and revisit conditions before/while building;
                                       AGENTS.md makes the conventions self-enforcing for agents.
                                       - **Multi-agent parallelism with lightweight coordination** — named
                                       workers, self-claimed tasks, worklog as shared memory; one syntax
                                       collision all day, and it surfaced a parser fact.
                                       - **Research-backed decisions** — web evidence settled the
                                       edit-format debate (D1) in minutes.
                                       - **Principles extracted from practice** — D4 came from watching a
                                       styled component fail to host real content, then was applied
                                       retroactively to the whole vocabulary.
                                       - **Dogfooding the product on itself** — the app's own chat sessions
                                       tested generation (demo Stacks/Tabs, an incremental edit patch,
                                       free-form "build me a dashboard" runs), and a screenshot of the
                                       rendered benchmark steered a fix.
                                       - **Timeboxing with an audit trail** — two-hour limit, break/resume
                                       entries, timestamped milestones; the time accounting in this review
                                       came straight from the log.

2026-07-08 03:44:32  ASSISTANT:        Added as **J2 — "Talk about the bundle,"** slotted right after J1
                                       since conversation naturally precedes (or replaces) generation;
                                       refine and keep renumbered to J3/J4, with TASKS.md and
                                       cross-references synced.

                                       The journey captures your framing: Q&A grounded in the wiki via the
                                       vault tools, answers that cite/link wiki pages, and — the key part —
                                       **fluid movement between purposes**: a study session becomes "quiz me
                                       on this" becomes a working artifact, without restarting the
                                       conversation. The definition-of-good encodes the study-notes scenario
                                       end to end.

                                       Two design implications worth noting now, because they change work
                                       already on the board:

                                       1. **Guidance needs a mode policy, not just artifact rules.**
                                       Everything in the wake-up prompt today is about *how to build
                                       artifacts*. J2 means the LLM also needs judgment about *when not to*:
                                       answer in chat vs. reach for the `ui` tool, how much to write in the
                                       small chat pane (your brevity TODO is really a J2 requirement), and
                                       when to proactively *offer* an artifact ("want me to turn this into a
                                       quiz?"). That's now explicit in the J2 task row.
                                       2. **It changes the ideal experiment corpus.** I'd asked whether J1's
                                       test bundle should be the project's own docs or a synthesized PR
                                       corpus — but a **study-notes wiki** now tests J1 *and* J2 in one
                                       session: discuss the notes (J2), then "quiz me" (J2→J1 handoff), then
                                       grade the artifact (J1). If you have real study notes lying around —
                                       or want me to draft a small set on a topic you'd genuinely enjoy
                                       being quizzed on — that might be the most honest test of the app's
                                       actual purpose. The PR-explainer corpus remains the best
                                       *archetype-fidelity* test, but it can be round two.

                                       Which corpus for the experiment — project docs, synthesized PR, or a
                                       study-notes set (and if so, on what)?

2026-07-08 04:33:17  RESULT:           [92 lines, 8912 chars; omitted about 7489 chars]
                                       first lines:
                                       1       # The ui tool
                                       2
                                       3       You can render a user interface into the main panel of the
                                       user's app
                                       4       by calling the `ui` tool (mcp__ui__ui) with a single `spec`
                                       argument
                                       5       containing an OpenUI Lang program, as specified below.
                                       last lines:
                                       90      - Structural components are named editing points
                                       (decisions.md D4): they render neutral layout only, with no visual
                                       styling of their own. Their value is that each named statement is an
                                       independently editable boundary — so decompose the page into many
                                       small named statements rather than a few large HTML blobs. Give the
                                       artifact its look with your own HTML and CSS: include a Content
                                       statement carrying a <style> block and target the components' hook
                                       classes (tabs-nav, tabs-trigger, gallery-nav-item, gallery-detail,
                                       aside-block, comparison-panel, comparison-label, ...) plus any
                                       className you set on instances.
                                       91      - Context-aware rendering: every component accepts an
                                       optional context prop (array of integers). A component without it
                                       always renders. A component with it renders only when the app's
                                       active context level is in its list. Level 0 always exists and is the
                                       app default; an exploration may introduce further levels 1, 2, 3, ...
                                       for different reader depths (e.g. 0 = new to the material, higher =
                                       more expert or more specialized). Emit one gated variant per level,
                                       include 0 on the variant that should show by default, and keep

                                       75-
                                       76-| Tool | Actions | Description |
                                       77-|---|---|---|
                                       --
                                       90:### 🛠️ Safe Editing
                                       91-- **`dryRun=true`**: Highly recommended before destructive
                                       operations like `delete` or `replace` with `replaceMode="section"`.
                                       92-- **Heading Disambiguation**: If multiple identical headings are
                                       found, the server returns `AMBIGUOUS_HEADING_TARGET` with a list of
                                       candidates. Use `blockId` to target specific elements if headings are
                                       not unique.
                                       93-- **AST vs Freeform**: Always prefer AST operations (`append`,
                                       `prepend`, `replace`, `delete`) as they are structural. Use
                                       `string_replace` only as a last resort; it requires exact literal
                                       matches including whitespace and newlines.
